METADATA PRODUCTION FOR NEWS IN TEXT, VIDEO, OR AUDIO?
PINs development and testing of AI systems for news took the needs of JP/Politikens Media Group as its point of departure. As JP/Politikens Media Group is mainly a text-driven news publisher, PIN focused on natural language processing (NLP), which is a text-focused AI technology, as the core method for metada-ta production. To address the needs of news publishers with core focus on video or audio, AI technologies such as computer vision, speech to text (STT) and text to speech (TTS) would likely have been more central to power metadata production.
7 5. KEY FINDINGS AND INSIGHTS In the process of developing and live-testing AI systems in JP/Politikens Media group, the PIN project produced knowledge about the challenges and opportunities related to the use of AI in news publishing. In this section, we describe key findings related to the four domains described in section 3, as well as insights related to processes and organization that cut across the domains. The findings stem from research carried out via the PIN-project and most has been published in the academic papers that are referenced below and listed together in section 8.
TECHNICAL FINDINGS AND INSIGHTS 1. AI technology is diverse and fast evolving AI technologies, ranging from text and image process-ing to personalization and predictive uses, are rapidly evolving. Core examples from the past four years include the maturing of AI-based language models (transformers) which in the early 2020s enabled ad-vancements in a certain type of recommender system (so-called content-based recommenders) which led to significant improvements in news personaliza-tion but also introduced new challenges related to potential audience fragmentation. Later in 2022, the emergence of the GPT3-generation of large language models made editorial tools based on generative AI feasible but also introduced new challenges related to e.g. content factuality which are particularly relevant in a news publishing context. This dynamic is likely to persist in the coming years, continuously blend-ing new opportunities and challenges for publishers stemming from AI developments.
2. AI systems must be adapted to the news domain AI systems solve the tasks they are designed for with a greater or lesser accuracy. For that reason, the types of AI systems that are used to recommend movies on Netflix or generate replies to customers in chatbots will often not work well for news publishers. Rather, making AI work for news publishing requires design-ing systems that take domain specific challenges such as the rapid pace of the news flow and the style of writing and minimal tolerance for factual errors in news articles into account. Examples of this (encountered on the PIN-project) include generative AI systems that must be prompted in certain ways to fit the editorial profile of news pub-lishers and implemented in certain ways (see “external grounding” below) to reduce the risks of factual errors which are particularly challenging in news coverage that strives to be factually correct. Another example was news personalization were the methods typically used in video streaming and e-commerce (so-called collaborative filtering recommenders) were found to fail in many news recommendation scenarios as they could not learn readers’ interests in newly published articles fast enough. Conversely, recommender sys-tems that personalize based on deep reader profiles and article metadata (so-called content-based recom-menders) were found able to effectively recommend articles news articles instantly when they are pub-lished and proved robust in most news recommenda-tion scenarios.
IMPLEMENTATION OF PINS AI SYSTEMS IN JP/POLITIKENS MEDIA GROUP Most of the AI systems developed by the PIN project were subsequently implemented at scale by Ekstra Bladet, including recommender systems for news flow personalization, multiple systems for metadata production, and a suite of genera-tive tools that assist journalists in the daily news production. In January 2024, JP/Politikens Media Group established a shared AI unit (JPPOL AI) tasked with rolling out AI systems from PIN across the group and continuing the development of new AI systems that build on PINs learnings and exploit the new AI technologies as they emerge. The first solutions based on technology from PIN that were rolled out across the group in collaboration with each news brands’ editorial rooms were certain AI systems for metadata-production and a suite of generative editorial tools called MAGNA (Monitoring and Assisted Generation of News Artefacts) that enables journalists to use genera-tive AI in news production in ways that align with each brands specific mission and tone of voice. 8 LIVE A/B TESTS OF RECOMMENDER SYSTEMS ON PIN PIN carried out more than live 70 A/B tests of numerous recommendation methods and non-personalized content selection meth-ods (e.g. article recommendations based on semantic search) in three phases that un-folded in 2021, 2022 and 2023. The results of these A/B tests – as well as data on the performance and effects of recommender systems rolled out at scale – are the basis for multiple insights in this report. Some of the results are more fully reported in Kruse et al 2023 while Kruse et al (2024a) and Kruse et al (2024b) reports the results of offline tests made by PIN as well as par-ticipants in ACM RecSys Challenge 2024 which was based on a news recommenda-tion dataset from PIN and included more than 200 researchers and data scientists from around the world (read more about the challenge on recsys.eb.dk) 3. New types of quality controls are needed While the technical systems that have tradition-ally enabled digital news publishing have at their foundation been logical, the machine learning that powers AI is fundamentally probabilistic. A consequence is that AI systems can perform tasks that go way beyond the capabilities of classical software systems, but also that AI systems can sometimes fail to predict the correct reader inter-ests or ‘hallucinate’, producing factually incorrect information. For that reason, AI requires new types of quality control and risk mediation that must take the special needs of the news domain into account. Avoiding factual mistakes, for example, is a key value in news publishing. As is well-known, the probabilistic nature of generative AI systems such as ChatGPT causes them to sometimes produce false information. However, challenges with hallucinations can be greatly reduced when generative AI is grounded in external facts such as news article drafts or news article archives. Nevertheless, even though such methods greatly reduce the risk of generative AI producing factual errors, editorial checks continue to be necessary to ensure correctness.
9 JOURNALISTIC FINDINGS AND INSIGHTS 4. AI technology is not neutral At the most basic level, AI technologies are not “neutral machines”. Rather, the choic-es that are made when AI systems are designed have great consequences for their performance and their effects on the production and distribution of news content. This applies to the AI systems that produce metadata which are sensitive to among other things the taxonomies and annotation protocols chosen by the systems de-signers (Thylstrup and Møller-Hartley 2024). It applies to generative systems which are sensitive to both choice of model (e.g. GPT4 or Claude 3) and the prompts used when generating content, as exemplified in insight #5 below. Further, it applies to recommender systems that produce very different news flows depending on choices made about what types of recommendation methods to use and how to train them. To exemplify the latter, the figure below shows how two fundamentally different rec-ommendation methods (collaborative filtering vs content-based) has the same ability to predict readers interests (measured by the users’ click-through-rate) but produces two very different news flows (measured by news topic shares in the personalized news flows).
5. Alignment with editorial profile requires constraining AI systems Aligning AI systems with editorial values require careful thought and often necessi-tates implementation of constraints that regulate the AI systems’ behavior. For that reason, editorial alignment of AI systems is still very much a human discipline that requires connecting situated knowledge of editorial values with technical skills in data science. Examples (encountered by PIN) are abounding: • The design of AI systems that produce metadata or evaluate the output of generative AI systems requires taxonomies and annotation protocols with strict definitions that necessitates deep knowledge of the news publishing domain as well as Danish cultural and social structures.
• News recommender systems, when left to optimize towards reader interests only, can easily deviate from a news publishers’ editorial profile which has traditionally always balanced readers interests against editorial values. On PIN, we found is was necessary to introduce a set of rules that constrained the types of news stories that a recommender system could recommend in a personalized news flow. Further, we designed the recommender systems to train on a users’ aggregate behavior on ekstrabladet.dk rather than only on the users’ interac-tion with the recommender system element to avoid a feedback loop that could increase audience fragmentation. • Generative AI systems tend to gravitate towards an average writing style rather than the tone of voice and journalistic angles of news publishers. We found that this can to some extent be addressed (via extensive prompting or fine tuning), as exemplified in the box below. However, aligning the output of generative language models with the editorial voice of specific news brands is likely be an ongoing challenge in generative editorial tools.

Integrating editorial values into AI systems is an ongoing challenge While the above exemplifies ways of integrating editorial values into AI systems such as recommender systems and generative AI sys-tems, they are still relatively crude and sometimes involve trade-offs between the accuracy of the systems and journalistic concerns. More elegant ways of integrating values in AI systems are, however, possible in principle, and alignment research is underway both in academia and industry that is likely to enable advances that can be exploited by news publishers, as exemplified in the box below.
ONGOING RESEARCH ON EDITORIAL ALIGNMENT OF RECOMMENDER SYSTEMS To encourage research in novel ways of integrating editorial values into news recommender systems, PIN and JP/Politikens Media Group open sourced a very large dataset for training and evaluation of news recommenders in early 2024. The dataset was used as the basis for the ACM RecSys Challenge 2024 where 200 academics and data scientists competed in devis-ing the best news recommendation methods. These were all evaluated for news flow effects. The challenge both showed that news recommenders ability to predict reader interests can be improved even further and confirmed PINs conclusions that recommender systems have significant and diverse news flow effects that require constraining their behavior to ensure editorial alignment and prevent audience fragmenta-tion. While the RecSys Challenge has concluded, the dataset is still available for research purposes (see recsys.eb.dk).
11 BUSINESS FINDINGS AND INSIGHTS 7. AI can increase the revenues of news publishers AI systems can increase the revenues of news publishers in multiple different ways when used in specific applications that affect the revenue drivers in news publishers’ business models. Examples include the use of AI-produced metadata to activate news content in new ways that increase engagement (e.g. in non-personalized “read more-lists” with related articles) and to generate semantic signals about readers’ interests that enabled more effective targeting of advertising.
